commands.review_summary:
The branch corrects Codex cached-input pricing and priority/long-context thresholds, adds a Codex cost formula version to the pricing key, regenerates the parser hash, and updates focused cost tests.

Reproducibility: yes. source-reproducible: current main bills `inputTokens` and `cachedInputTokens` additively even though cached input is a subset, and the linked issue plus PR tests/proof cover the cached-heavy Codex cost path. I did not run Swift tests because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: - No automated repair is needed; the remaining action is maintainer review of the intentional cache reprice and normal merge/check handling.

target.title: Fix Codex cost inflation from cached input double-billing
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes Codex cost inflation (≈2.7x–4.6x depending on workload) for any usage that includes cached input tokens. The regression was introduced by #1768 ("Fix Codex archived usage undercount") and is present on `main`; it is not in any shipped release (the last release, `v0.37.2`, predates that merge).

The archived-usage dedupe part of #1768 (`seenSessionIds` -> `contributingSessionIds`) is correct and is left untouched. This change only fixes the pricing math and the cache invalidation that lets the fix take effect.

Fixes #1796.

## Root cause

`Sources/CodexBarCore/Vendored/CostUsage/CostUsagePricing.swift`, private `codexCostUSD(pricing:...)`. Codex reports `input_tokens` as the total prompt size, with `cached_input_tokens` a subset of it. #1768 changed the function to treat them as additive:

```diff
-        let nonCached = max(0, inputTokens - cached)
+        let nonCached = max(0, inputTokens)
-        let usesLongContextRates = pricing.thresholdTokens.map { max(0, inputTokens) > $0 } ?? false
+        let usesLongContextRates = pricing.thresholdTokens.map { (nonCached + cached) > $0 } ?? false
```

Two compounding defects:

1. Cached tokens are double-billed: the full `input_tokens` is charged at the input rate, and the cached subset is charged again at the cache-read rate.
2. The 272K long-context threshold is evaluated against `input + cached` (double-counting cached) instead of `input`, so workloads tip into the doubled long-context rates far too early.

## Fix

1. Restore the clamp in `codexCostUSD`: bill only the non-cached remainder at the input rate, and key the long-context threshold off `inputTokens` (which already includes cached).

```swift
let cached = min(max(0, cachedInputTokens), max(0, inputTokens))
let nonCached = max(0, inputTokens - cached)
...
let usesLongContextRates = pricing.thresholdTokens.map { max(0, inputTokens) > $0 } ?? false
```

2. Cache invalidation. Costs are persisted per file as precomputed nanos (`codexCostNanos`) and only repriced when `codexPricingKey` changes. That key is derived from rate constants only, so a formula-only fix would keep reusing stale inflated costs for already-scanned days. A `codexCostFormulaVersion` token is added to the hashed input of `codexPricingKey` (both the built-in and models.dev branches, prefixes preserved) so existing caches mismatch and reprice via the existing full-rescan path.

## Tests

- Reverted the expectations in `CostUsagePricingTests`, `CostUsageScannerPriorityTests`, and `CostUsageScannerBreakdownTests` that #1768 changed to assert the inflated numbers.
- Added `does not double bill cached input tokens` and `clamps cache reads to input tokens` in `CostUsagePricingTests`.
- Added `codex daily report reprices cached costs when cost formula version changes` in `CostUsageScannerBreakdownTests`, which seeds a cache with a stale pricing key and inflated nanos and asserts it reprices to the correct value.

Commands run:

```
swift test --filter CostUsagePricingTests --filter CostUsageScannerPriorityTests --filter CostUsageScannerBreakdownTests --filter CostUsageFetcherTests --filter PiSessionCostScannerTests --filter CostUsageDailyReportMergeTests
make check
```

All pass (124 cost tests; 0 lint violations).
